Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]
Moriya Shrine Torii {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Kawagishi Higashi 1-12 |coordinates = {{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}} |type = {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}} |創建 = Unknown |alternative names = Moriya Daimyōjin, Moriyama Daimyōjin, Moriya Ōkami, Moriya Shrine |festival = October 20 (Currently the third Sunday in October) }} '''Moriya Shrine''' (読み仮名) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Nagano Prefecture, in the Kawagishi Higashi Hashiwara district (formerly {{ill|Kawagishi Village|ja|川岸村|橋原村}}). The shrine's former classification under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}} was that of a {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}}. == Overview == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}, a deity from {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪地方}} traditions. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]

A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]
Moriya Shrine Torii {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Kawagishi Higashi 1-12 |coordinates = {{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}} |type = {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}} |創建 = Unknown |alternative names = Moriya Daimyōjin, Moriyama Daimyōjin, Moriya Ōkami, Moriya Shrine |festival = October 20 (Currently the third Sunday in October) }} '''Moriya Shrine''' (読み仮名) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Nagano Prefecture, in the Kawagishi Higashi Hashiwara district (formerly {{ill|Kawagishi Village|ja|川岸村|橋原村}}). The shrine's former classification under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}} was that of a {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}}. == Overview == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}, a deity from {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪地方}} traditions. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]
Moriya Shrine Torii {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Kawagishi Higashi 1-12 |coordinates = {{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}} |type = {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}} |創建 = Unknown |alternative names = Moriya Daimyōjin, Moriyama Daimyōjin, Moriya Ōkami, Moriya Shrine |festival = October 20 (Currently the third Sunday in October) }} '''Moriya Shrine''' (読み仮名) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Nagano Prefecture, in the Kawagishi Higashi Hashiwara district (formerly {{ill|Kawagishi Village|ja|川岸村|橋原村}}). The shrine's former classification under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}} was that of a {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}}. == Overview == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}, a deity from {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪地方}} traditions. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]
Moriya Shrine Torii {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Kawagishi Higashi 1-12 |coordinates = {{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}} |type = {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}} |創建 = Unknown |alternative names = Moriya Daimyōjin, Moriyama Daimyōjin, Moriya Ōkami, Moriya Shrine |festival = October 20 (Currently the third Sunday in October) }} '''Moriya Shrine''' (読み仮名) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Nagano Prefecture, in the Kawagishi Higashi Hashiwara district (formerly {{ill|Kawagishi Village|ja|川岸村|橋原村}}). The shrine's former classification under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}} was that of a {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}}. == Overview == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}, a deity from {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪地方}} traditions. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''Annual Festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]

The shrine's former classification under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}} was that of a {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}}. == Overview == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}, a deity from {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪地方}} traditions. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Moriya Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Moriya Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]
Moriya Shrine Torii {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Kawagishi Higashi 1-12 |coordinates = {{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}} |type = {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}} |創建 = Unknown |alternative names = Moriya Daimyōjin, Moriyama Daimyōjin, Moriya Ōkami, Moriya Shrine |festival = October 20 (Currently the third Sunday in October) }} '''Moriya Shrine''' (読み仮名) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Nagano Prefecture, in the Kawagishi Higashi Hashiwara district (formerly {{ill|Kawagishi Village|ja|川岸村|橋原村}}). The shrine's former classification under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}} was that of a {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}}. == Overview == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}, a deity from {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪地方}} traditions. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Moriya Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Moriya Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]] [[Category:ppp]]

{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Moriya Shrine |image = [[File:洩矢神社（岡谷市）- Moriya Shrine (Okaya).jpg|280px]]
Moriya Shrine Torii {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E}}}} |location = {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}, {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Kawagishi Higashi 1-12 |coordinates = {{coord|36|03|04|N|138|02|10|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}} |type = {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}} |創建 = Unknown |alternative names = Moriya Daimyōjin, Moriyama Daimyōjin, Moriya Ōkami, Moriya Shrine |festival = October 20 (Currently the third Sunday in October) }} '''Moriya Shrine''' (読み仮名) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|ja|岡谷市}}, Nagano Prefecture, in the Kawagishi Higashi Hashiwara district (formerly {{ill|Kawagishi Village|ja|川岸村|橋原村}}). The shrine's former classification under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was that of a {{ill|Village shrine (Japan)|ja|村社}}. == Overview == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}, a deity from {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪地方}} traditions. Its exact date of establishment is unknown. The shrine currently stands approximately 200 meters from the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}}, though it was originally said to be located by the riverbank. Archaeological evidence suggests that the area surrounding the shrine has been inhabited since the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Near the shrine's southwestern side lies the "Kyōzuka (Kyōzukahara) Site" {{Cite book|和書|year=1980|title=長野県中央道埋蔵文化財包蔵地発掘調査報告書 岡谷市その4 昭和52・53年度|editor=長野県中央道遺跡調査団|publisher=[[Japan Highway Public Corporation]] Nagoya Construction Bureau, [[Nagano Prefectural Board of Education]]|chapter=第2節 経塚（経塚原）遺跡|pages=55-96|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/en/8040}}, once believed to be the dwelling of {{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}.{{Cite book|和書|year=1924|title=諏訪史 第1巻|author=Torii Ryūzō|authorlink=Torii Ryūzō|publisher=Shinano Educational Society Suwa Branch|pages=373-374|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/935820/373}} == deity == * '''{{ill|Moreya|lt=Moriya no Kami|ja|洩矢神}}''' : The local {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} of the former Hashiwara Village and a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, historically associated with the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Upper Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#上社}}. == History == According to the {{ill|Takeminakata#Mythological entry into Suwa|ja|タケミナカタ#明神入諏|Suwa Myths}}, Moriya no Kami, an indigenous {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神}}, opposed the advance of {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|建御名方神}} (the Suwa deity) into Suwa. Following a battle, Moriya no Kami was defeated and submitted to Takeminakata, becoming his vassal and the progenitor of the {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}, who served the Suwa Upper Shrine.Miyasaka Mitsuaki, ''Suwa Taisha no Onbashira to Nenchū Gyōji'' (郷土出版社, 1992), pp. 91-93.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nagano-jinjacho.jp/shibu/04nansin/02suwa/4030.html|title=Moriya Shrine|website=Nagano Shrine Association|accessdate=2018-10-01}} Medieval texts locate the confrontation between the two deities near {{ill|Mount Moriya|ja|守屋山}}, around the {{ill|Upper Shrine Main Sanctuary|ja|諏訪大社#上社|本宮}} area. However, later Edo-period traditions identify the {{ill|Tenryū River|ja|天竜川}} banks as their battleground. Today, Moriya Shrine and nearby {{ill|Fujishima Shrine (Suwa)|ja|藤島神社 (諏訪地方)}} are recognized as the sites of their respective camps.{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1960|title=Suwa Monogatari|publisher=Kōyō Shobō|pages=3-15|chapter=Moriya no Kami and Takeminakata}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Imai Nokiku|year=1976|title=The Land of the Gods: Ancient Suwa Tales|publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai|pages=41-43}}{{Cite book|和書|author=Moriya Sanae|year=2017|title=The Guide to the Chino Moriya Archive|edition=Third Edition|pages=2-3|editor=Chino Moriya Archive|editor-link=神長官守矢史料館}} It is said that the shrine originally stood in Hanoka Village (now Okaya's Minato district). When residents moved to Hashiwara Village, they brought Moriya Shrine with them.{{Cite book|和書|editor=Hanoka District History Committee|year=1983|title=Hanoka District History|chapter=民間信仰と伝承|pages=620,637}} === The Fuji of the Tenryū River === A legend recounts that Moriya Shrine was once located by the riverbank. Wisteria trees in its precincts grew intertwined with those across the river at Fujishima Shrine, creating a bridge-like canopy. During the Kanbun era (1661–1673), the {{ill|Suwa Domain|ja|諏訪藩}} lord ordered their removal for a firefly-viewing event. However, local villagers feared divine retribution and refused. A man named Koishi Kaemon took on the task for a fee but soon fell into madness and met a tragic end, further cementing the wisteria’s sacred status. == Worship and Rituals == * [[Genshi-sai]] (元始祭) * {{ill|Insect-Sending Festival|ja|虫送り}} (虫送り祭) * '''festival''' (festival): October 20 or the third Sunday in October * [[Ninenmairi]] (二年参り): December 31 * '''Onbashira Festival''' (御柱祭): Conducted in years of the Tiger and Monkey : Historically aligned with the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社}} festival but now held on separate dates. == Access == * 15 minutes (1.1 km) on foot from {{ill|Okaya Station|ja|岡谷駅}}, JR {{ill|Chūō Main Line|ja|中央本線}} * 1 minute from "Moriya Shrine Front" bus stop, {{ill|Silky Bus Kawagishi Line|ja|シルキーバス川岸橋原線}} == Related Sites == === Former Moriya Ōkami Site === Located 300m from the shrine, marked by a stone monument with calligraphy by Moriya Jikken, the 75th shrine chief. === Ryūgen Mountain deity === A small shrine about 1.3 km from Moriya Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ōyama-tsumi|ja|大山祇神}}. The area was once part of Moriya no Kami’s sphere of influence. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [https://moriyajinja.amebaownd.com/ Official Moriya Shrine Website] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Suwa Faith}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Moriya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha]] [[Category:History of Okaya, Nagano]] [[Category:Tenryū River]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}[[ja:洩矢神社]][[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Moriya Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Moriya Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Moriya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Morikawa Shrine]] [[Category:ppp]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]]
